OURRENT AFYAIRS.

The encmics of Home Rule are making a poor show
- row thet the Bill i bofore the country.  The ¢ Irish
- Times”” was all on' the ‘“ who struck O'Hara '’ pose
before the Bill was brought in; now that the Bill is
 before it, it is struck almost dumb. The Convention
apparently has given the ‘‘rebel '’ *‘Independent
use. But in due course that paper and the *‘ Irish
Times '’ will, no doubt, regain their breath. What the
-Home Rulers in this country want is something to
e upon. Even the man in the cellar, not to men-
e man in the street, can see through people who
same breath are declaring that the finances of
re bad for Ireland whilst they are robbery for

The Junior Branch of the Trish Unionist Alliance held
o meeting in Dublin last week—certainly the branch
'y junior in the way of intellect. Though *‘junior,
old fogics like W. Treland, the off-licensed Bung, and
Mr. Samuels, K.C., were amongst the orators. Even
that meeting helped to show how hopelessly muddled
the anti-FHome Rulers are—they have yet to arrive at a
lie agreed upon. One speaker, a chap named Cusack,
said i—
After six or seven years the Nationalist Party,
if the Bill passed, would have everything in their
own hands.

is very

Another speaker, a person named Maddock, said :—
It did not require a student of politics to see
that the Bill was a stepping stone to an Irish Re-
public.

Another man, or boy, named Clegg, said :—
The Bill was an insult to all the Ivish people
who had an interest in the country.

Mu., or Master, Weir Johnston, said:—

The National Convention showed that the vast
body of delegates did not appreciate the effect of
the Bill as offered to them, as Mr. Redmond had
said, as a final settlement. As it at present stood
it was an insult to the word National.

Mr. A. W. Samuels, K.C., evidently did not agree with
the gentleman who predicted the Republic, for he
said -—
He could tell them, after considerable delibera-
tion, if this Bill passed into law it would be a de-
solate and bankrupt country in a few years.

The anti-Home Rulers have not yet agreed upon the
anti-Home Rule cry—or we might say lie: and so they
are speaking with divers contradictory tongues.

There are more things in a nation than can be ex-
pressed in a ledger account. Of course you can carry
disregard for mere finance tqo far on the ‘one side as you
can carry disregard for everything but finance too far
on the other side. Beware of those who attempt to
express the Irish nation in terms of money on two pages
of a penny pass book: it can’t be done.

We are not paying our way now, notwithstanding the
hundreds of millions of over-taxation we have paid since
the Union. It is to our interest to get a reasonable
freedom: it is now to England’s interest to limit its
loss on the running of Treland. We might possibly
prove more expensive to England in the near future if
the present arrangement lasted, but the freedom that
the Home Rule Bill offers is a better bargain for us, -
and it incidentally limits England’s losses. The Bill is
favourable to Ireland and to England, and that is its
strong point and that is what is confounding the enemy.

The Castle-hunting Englishman, Mr. George Flet-
cher, who draws a large salary out of the technical side
of the Department, has been lecpuring in London. He
would be better employed working in Ireland, from
which country he draws his exorbitant salary. Accord-
ing to a Press report, he said:—'‘ They were desirous -
from the first to develop a system' of technical educa-
tion 'in harmony with the peculiar needs of Ireland in
o manner best calculated to further its industrial pro-
gress.”” Talk is cheap. What about the gas industry ?

MAY MORNING ON A HILL.

Below the rath of Ballyduff
The aspen silver grey

Is shaking, shaking, cold enough
On this the first of May.,

Tho’ rain is on the bramble hill
And morning sky is dark,

The airy chill, the clouds that drop,
Have failed to still or stay or stop
The music of a lark.

Crows are calling, rain is falling,
Hurry down the winding glown—
Down the valley, fresh and mellow,
Gorse’s scented, blossoms yellow;
Alders growing in the hollow,
Streamlet flowing, we must follow,
Where among the pebbles grey

All the day its ripples tinkling
Down the hill and thro’ the vale.
Primrose pale and mosses sprinkling;
In the vale and on the hill,

Grey and chill the aspens quiver,
By the river’s rushy pool.

Dark and cool, let us go

Where the sloe and drinaun dhown,
With a crown of blossoms white,
Smiling thro’ the dew of night,
Welcome in the summer bright.

On- Sunday, May 5th, a charity sermon in aid of
Our Lady’s Hospice for the Dying, Harold’s Cross,
Dublin, will be preached by the Very Rev. Michael A.
Kelly, C.S.Sp., in the Carmelite Church, Whitefriar
Street, after the first Gospel of the last Mass. The
claims of the Hospice for the Dying appeal to every
generous heart, and those of our readers who cannot be
present at the Carmelite Church on May 5th may send
their subseriptions to his Grace the Archbishop, the
priests of the parish or of Whitefriar Street, or to any
Convent of the Sisters of Charity in Ireland, or direct
to the Hospice, Harold’s Cross.

Why does not the Dublin Tramway Co. get its tickets
printed in Ireland?  Surely when William Martin
Murphy’s “‘ Independent ’’ pretends to be so keenly
patriotic concerning the Home Rule Bill, Mr. Murphy
at least might see that the printing of the tramway
tickets was done in Ireland. We have seen an appli-
cation from a London firm for an advertisement to an
Trish firm setting out that the former had to print a
large number of- tickets for the Dublin Trams.

We are ‘‘ fed up,’’ as the Johnnies say, with William
O’Brien, the Suffers, and Horace Plunkett. Why nobt
these bores give the counfry a rest for a while? In
these days a two column letter from the head of Plun-
kett House does not stand much chance of being widely
read ; we read it as part of our work. Poor Sir Horace
never seems to get much for'ader; he is always
groping about, and muttering down in, the foundations;

" he is perpetually chewing ‘‘ root principles.”” Too much
of any root, even of ‘‘roob principles,”” is nauseating.
The farmers of Ireland, besides the advantages accruing

THE LEADER.'

Has this Dublin Castle hunter attempted to do any-
thing in the way of helping the education of Irishmen

Mav 4, 1912.

from Land Purchase and the Congested Districts Board,
have a highly endowed Department of Agriculture ab
their service. We have no doubt what the work
of the Department of Agriculture has been, and
is, o vast help to them. After all, however people may

criticise the Department or be dissatisfied wibh i, it 1s -
simply impossible that such a heavily endowed machine .

of State help for farmers could exist without being of
vast help and service to the farming industry. Land
Purchase, the Congested Districts Board, and the De-
partment are the three great forces beneficently oper-
ating on the farmers’ industry. '\s for co-olpcl'utlon,
about thirty thousand pounds sterling of public money
has already been spent on it, and it was also the reci-
pient of a very large sum from other sources: it boasts
of having ** 100,000 organised farmers.” Surely it is
now for the farmers to go ahead and co-operate to their
heart’s content if they are that way inclined, and no
one can say them nay. They have Land Purchase, the
Congested Districts Board, the heavily endowed and
highly organised Department of Agriculture, and there
are alleged to be 100,000 of them organised in the co-
operative business. Surely that is sufficient push off
for them. What more do they want as farmers? We
are not aware that they, as farmers, are asking for any
more.

This eternal mumbling about ‘‘ root principles,’”
‘* foundations,”” and things that may have been fresh
fifteen years ago, is very boring. It appears there is
some scheme or plan for promoting co-operation
amongst farmers in England and Scotland. Whether
or not farmers in Ingland and Scotland attach any
importance to these aspirations, backed by mild and
conditioned State finance, is more than we can say,
and the matter does not interest us in the least. But
the fact, so far as Ireland is concerned, is that about
£30,000 of State imoney, and a large amount of contri-
buted money, has been, during many years, devoted to
this work, and, as a result, those interested say that
they have 100,000 organised farmers! What more do
they want? Co-operation, on their own showing, has
been put on its feet. If there is any State money going
to which Ireland could make a claim to a share, by all
means let her get it. DBut as co-operation has been
placed on its feet in Ireland, partly by the aid of about
£30,000 State funds, it seems to us the commonsense
ike thing to suggest is that whilst England and Scotland
may look for funds to help them in the inifial work of
agricultural co-operation, Ireland, having established
co-operation on a wide basis, should seek her share of
the money for other purposes, such as forestry and so
orth. (By the way, last week, by a printer’s error,
the *‘ about £80,000 " of State money used up by the
.A.0.8. was printed as ‘‘ about £300,000."")

This controversy has its sordid and mean side. We
can understand the anxiety of the hangers-on of the
.A.0.8. to get a State subsidy to go into the till out
of which their salaries come. Plunkett, it is true,
being a wealthy man, is not personally interested in a
State subsidy, but greed of power, patronage, and per-
sonal vanity may be quite as mean and sordid as greed
of gold. No matter with what fine, Vague words f’luu-
kett surrounds the issue the plain fact remains that
T. W. Russell, a wily Seotchman, was smart and slim
enough to *“ pinch ”’—as John Bull, Jun., would put it
—Plunkett’s job. That, it seems to us, is T. W. Rus-
sell’s unforgiveable sin. That the money attached to

Concerning Gaelic in Belfast University, ‘“ B.”’
writes :i—"‘ The other evening a most enjoyable lecture
was given in the Co Comghall, Belfast College of
Irish, on the Irish Scale System in its relafion to our
old folk songs by Mr. Reynolds, who is one of the most
eminent musical critics we have in the country. That
stalwart Gael, Mr. Francis Jos. I er, presided, and
took oceasion to announce the gratifying news that our
ancient language has at length received its proper status
in Belfast University, inasmuch as the Lectureship in
Trish has been raised to the dignity of a Chair. We
learn that Professor Henry in the Queen’s has been
untiring in his efforts for this victory, and has been
ably seconded by Mr. John Burke, Belfast. The latter
has also been instrumental in securing a course of Irish
for the curriculum of the summer lectures for students.
So, what with the crowded benches of the Colaiste
Comghall, and the recognition of Gaelic in its University

* Chair, Irish is booming in Belfast just now.”

We read in a local paper:—

On Friday morning last an old woman named
Cooke, aged 86, with her son and the latter’s wife
and child, were evicted from a holding on the Farn-
ham Estate at Portlongfield, near Killeshandra.

Mr. Travers R. Blackley, J.P., Lord Farnham'’s
agent, attended, accompanied by the Sheriff’s
bailiffs and six policemen.

There were only a few children present at the
eviction.

Mr. Michael - Reilly, R.O., Lahard, on whom
forty-eight hours’ notice of the eviction had been
served, visited the place and found the old woman
Iying on a heap of rags outside the old homestead.

“T’lIl die herz on these stones,”” she declared,
‘“ and Lord Farnham will be responsible. It’s bad
gratitude for. the daughter of John Noble, my
father, who was one of the men who had the honour
of ‘ chairing ’ another Lord Farnham at the elec-
tion of 1826. He got a piece of the decoration of
the chair and had it made into a neck-tie which

" he wore at the 12th July demonstrations from
that time until the day of his death, and I have
it still as a souvenir. Ah, where are the Orange-
men now 2’ the old woman cried—‘* where is the
English Government, or will they leave a poor old
woman of 86 years to die on these stones? Ts this
the gratitude we were told about ?”

This appears to be a very curious case. Where were
the Orangemen ? Where was Cavson’s army.?

A HOME RULE NATIONAL ANTHEM.

(Recommended for practice to Irish Nationalists, who,
unlike Matty Bodkin and a lot of others, are unused
to loyal harmony.)

Deep from her hosom’s core

Let Treland’s anthem soar,
+ God save the King.

Ireland victorious,

Happy and glorious,

Sings out uproarious,

God save the King.

Long venal loyalty

Sang songs of royalty

Ireland to sting.

Long may the aliens reign,
Slaves may the Gaels remain,
Spoke in the loyal strain,
God save the King.

From greedy Iaction free,
Ireland from sea to sea,

On Freedom’s wing,

With feelings not for sale,
Gladly the King may hail.
Long may he rule the Gael.
God save the King.

We’ll make him Ireland’s own
And guard his royal throne
From treason’s ring.
Laugh we at Carson’s frown,
Down, Ulster rebels, down,
Hands off our liege and crown.
God save the King.
A. M. W.

. When the study of Irish was being agitated one of
the contentions brought against it was what we may
call the pint pot theory of education. It was urged
that only so much could fit into the head, and that the
putting in of Irish meant as a consequence the letting
out of some knowledge of corresponding volume. The
effect of Irish on the Irish brain was left out of ac-
count: its stimulating qualities, its bracing and steady-
ing effect on character and on the national life and
energy were all ignored by the wise pint potters. We
observe a somewhat similar attitude of mind towards
Home Rule. Some people seem to think that Treland
represents an unvarying output of industrial energy,
and that Home Rule merely proposes some new sums
in division. We have seen the great material effect—
to go no further—that the Irish Ireland movement has
had in a few years. We confess that we look for the
greatest material good in Home Rule to its bracing and
stimulating effect on Irish character, intellect and
energy. Ireland is not a pint pot that contains a given
amount and no more : she is a living orgamsm capable
of comparatively vast and rapid development. She is
rather an untilled field: she has gone partly fallow
owing to forces militating against her. ~ We look to
Home Rule to dispel these forces and create a new force
that will brace up her energies.

We have often expressed the view that so far as the
agricultural problem is concerned what is wanted is
that the farmers’ heads should be tilled : the same ap-
plies all round in Ireland: the business men need to
have their heads tilled just as much as the farmers.
We want a quickening of life all round and that quick-
ening Home Rule will give to a considerable extent.
The hordes of small Marshalls and Snellgroves who are
out with their pencils totting up figures and making
percentages are very well meaning in their way, but it
must not be forgotten that they are only Marshalls and
Snellgroves all the time. Ireland is not a drapery
shop ; it is a nation.

The City of the Confederation, otherwise the hamlet
of *“ marble and mud,” is not the only place where the
circumstances of a Catholic social entertainment calls
for comment. - The primatial city of Armagh was re-
cently the scene of a ball—the Armagh Catholic Ladie
Leap Year Ball. There were no Irish dances at this
ball, but there was what was called a **twilight dance,”’
whatever that is. The catering was given to the Grand

Master of the Armagh Orangemen. Is that tolevance
proving ?  Surely the Nationalists are not so backward

in Armagh that one of them could not efficiently cater
for a Jeap year ball with a “twilight dance.””  We hear
that all the unmarried members of the R.I.C. and a
few soldiers were invited to this select ball with the
** twilight dance.”” We would like to hear a little more
-about this tony and fashionable function.

Kenilworth Pharmacy at 2 Kenilworth DBuildings,
Harold’s Cross Road, Rathgar. Mr. T. Keawell is well

doing so at the Kenilworth Pharmacy. We wish Mr.
Keawell great success in his new enterprise.

- “ DISCUSSING "’ THE BILL.

- The poor Unionists and the *‘ Irish Times '’ are in

- o bad way for an effective cry against the Home Rule
Bill. The leading article in last Saturday’s * Irish
Times * stated that it would be a pity if the National-
ists refused to study the report of the meeting of the
“Irish Unionist Alliance: it said that several of the
speakers had ‘‘ expert’’ Lnowledge of their subject.
We turned to the report of this Irish Unionist Alliance
meeting expecting to read some stimulating opposition
to Home Rule, but we found little but froth and non-
sense in their speeches. We see that Lord Farnham
was there, and so was Siv John Arnott, and amongst
those who apologised for not being present was M.
Pierce Gun Mahony.

The speakers were driven headlong into raimeis. A
M. Powell, K.C., said the Bill *‘ would not satisfy the
national aspirations of a colony of niggers.”” Mr. Denny
Heury, K.C., was rather unhappy from his supporters’
point of view by stating how melancholy he felt. *‘ He
said that it was a somewhat melancholy duty at this
hour of the day to have to rise and address such a
meeting, gathered in defence of the most elementary
principles of freedom and liberty.”” And yet, accord-
ing to the previous spealker, this menace to ‘‘the most
elementary principles of freedom and liberty *’ is a Bill
that ** would not satisfy the national aspirations of a
colony of niggers.”” Certainly the anti-Irish party in
Ireland ought to make an attempt to level up their
oratory. Surely even Unionists of average sense must
turn up their noses with disgust at such ranting as was
indulged in at the Irish Unionist Alliance. For in-
stance, Denny Henry, K.C., asked :—'‘ What would
be the result when the heads of the League and their
subordinates were put over the heads of all the hones
members of the community ?”’ The suggestion ap-
pears to be that the heads and subordinates of the
United Trish League are all scoundrels and that there
ave no honest members of the community but Union-
ists! That sort of oratory is simply silly, and it is
regrettable to see even Irish Unionists indulging in it.
Who is Sir Malby Crofton, Bart.? He made one par-
ticular remark in which there is a considerable amount
of truth. He said:—'‘ He really did not care what
the laws of a country were so long as they were ad-
ministered properly.’”’ Everyone has his own view of
what “ properly no doubt so long as the Ascen-
dancy had the administering of the law they felt satis-
fied enough: but even if Home Rule failed Sir Malby
Crofton may say farewell to the old Ascendancy days.
Even the denial of Home Rule cannot keep the Nation-
alists, the mere Irish, from tending strongly and effec-
tively to reach their level in this country. TLord Mid-
leton must be a moderate and courteous gentleman, for
he actually said :—*‘ They were not there to say that
everything that Mr. Dillon and Mr. Redmond had told
them for the last twenty years was a lie.”” How nice
and condescending. And that is the sort of stuff that
Naticnalists ave asked to read and study.

The cry of an attempt to stifle discussion surely will
not bear examination.  Who can stifle it? And surely
“in the name of free discussion a man is entitled to pub
forward the view that too much indiscriminate discus-
sion ought to be avoided. Tt is exercising free discus-
sion {o express the view that enemies of Home Rule
would be glad to promote and encourage discussion
with a view to creating wrangling and disunity. No

- one, so long as the owner wishes it, can stop free dis-
cussion on Home Rule in the ‘ Independent,”” and on

FATHER KENNEDY MEMORIAL.

HE widespread manifestation of sorrow witnessed

on the occasion of the funeral of Father Kennedy

is evidence that in his untimely death not merely the

parish of Fermoy and the diocese of Cloyne, but thp
whole country suffered grievous loss. e i

The people of Fermoy—where he ministered (I_urm.’s’
the last ten years of his life, and to which, when stricken
with fatal illness, he returned to die—were not moved
by more vehement emotions of sorrow than the pcople
of Meelin, Youghal and Blarney, among whom the earlier
ye: ol his pricsthood were spent. x

I'rom the beginning of his ministry to its tragic close
he had everywhere a double title to the aﬁcctionlo['thl
people—his priestly perfection and his fearless patriotism.

Wherever he served he is remembered by the poor
for his boundless charity to them, and by rich and poor
alike as a zealous priest, a wise counsellor, a devoted
Iriend. Wherever he served, too, he threw himsell into
the people’s struggle to overthrow oppression, to secure
better laws, to bring about a better system of Govern-
ment. i

Throughout the wide extent of Cork County, fr_om
Meelin to Youghal, there was none more unsparing” in
his exertions, more fearless in courage, more boldfin
action, duri the prolonged fight against landlordism
and the Government that supported it, even its excesses,
‘than Father Kennedy.

His repeated terms of imprisonment only increased
the determination to carry on the fight. His strategy
circumvented the enen is unearthing and publishing
the most secret instructions from the Castle shamed
the Government into cancelling their most brutal orders.

When the people’s victory was won, when the farmers
were secured in their holdings, and houses were provided
for the labourers, he continued to take a prominent part
in the struggle for an Irish Parliament. To the end of
his life he was an honoured and valued adviser in the
Councils of the Nation. How sad that he should not
be spared to witness the success of that struggle for -
Home Rule in which he laboured so consistently, so
unselfishly, so unsparingly !

The prayers of the poor and of the oppressed will he
always his; but his numerous friends throughout the
country insist that the occasion demands something
more. A meeting in Fermoy resolved that a suitable
memorial he erected, and appointed a committee to give
effect to the general desire. And the members of this
committee appeal with confidence to the generosity of
their fellow-countrymen for subscriptions to enable them
to erect for-this devoted priest -and ardent Irishman
& memorial that will be worthy of him and of the cause
for which he laboured and suffered and died.

All subscriptions must be sent to the hon. treasurer,
who will gratefully acknowledge same.
